Task id: T72 (ledgerRefs goals:G5)
Base commit: d1c32f3 (main) — your worktree may be cut from an older HEAD; FIRST run `git reset --hard d1c32f3` inside YOUR worktree (sanctioned base-refresh), then `git switch -c implement/T72` so your commit lands on the task branch.

NOTE: this is a Go repository (wanbond). The check gate is `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...` via `nix develop -c` if go is not on PATH (per AGENTS.md); there is no bun here. internal/dnsresolve already ships SystemResolver, DoHResolver (NewDoHResolver), DoTResolver (NewDoTResolver) behind a Resolver seam.

headline: Add the [dns] config block selecting resolver mode, cadence, and timeouts

description: New top-level `[dns]` block in internal/config: `resolver = "system" | "doh" | "dot"` (default system), `doh_url` (required iff doh), `dot_server` (host:port, required iff dot), `poll_interval` (re-resolution cadence, duration string, sane default on the reconcile-cadence scale per Q31, validated > 0), `timeout` (per-lookup bound, default a few seconds). BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant (chicken-and-egg): the DoH/DoT server address must itself be reachable WITHOUT a DNS lookup — require the doh_url host / dot_server to be an IP literal, or require an explicit `bootstrap_ip` field when a hostname is used; validation FAILS FAST otherwise (a plaintext lookup of the private resolver's own name would defeat Q33's purpose). Validation: mode-specific required fields; reject DoH/DoT settings when resolver=system; an absent block is inert (system defaults) — the per-peer opt-in flag remains the sole gate, [dns] merely selects transport. Provide a constructor mapping the validated block onto the internal/dnsresolve implementations. Zero-value behavior: absent block == system resolver defaults, still gated by the per-peer flag.

acceptance: go test ./internal/config/... passes a validation matrix: absent block yields system defaults; doh without doh_url fails; dot without dot_server fails; a hostname-form doh_url/dot_server without bootstrap_ip fails fast with a clear error; poll_interval <= 0 fails; a full valid doh and dot block each construct the matching dnsresolve implementation in a unit test.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	package config
2	
3	import (
4		"encoding/base64"
5		"encoding/hex"
6		"errors"
7		"fmt"
8		"math"
9		"net"
10		"net/netip"
11		"strconv"
12		"strings"
13		"time"
14	
15		awgdevice "github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/device"
16	)
17	
18	// Role selects which end of the tunnel this process runs as. It is an explicit,
19	// required field; the role is never inferred from other configuration.
20	type Role string
21	
22	const (
23		// RoleEdge is the mobile Linux box that bonds the WAN uplinks.
24		RoleEdge Role = "edge"
25		// RoleConcentrator is the public-IP VPS that terminates the tunnel.
26		RoleConcentrator Role = "concentrator"
27	)
28	
29	func (r Role) valid() bool {
30		return r == RoleEdge || r == RoleConcentrator
31	}
32	
33	// keyLen is the byte length of a Curve25519 WireGuard key and of a raw
34	// outer-control PSK, before base64 encoding.
35	const keyLen = 32
36	
37	// Config is the whole wanbond configuration, shared by both roles and parsed
38	// from a single TOML file.
39	type Config struct {
40		Role      Role            `toml:"role"`
41		Paths     []Path          `toml:"paths"`
42		WireGuard WireGuard       `toml:"wireguard"`
43		Amnezia   Amnezia         `toml:"amnezia"`
44		PSK       Key             `toml:"psk"`
45		Metrics   Metrics         `toml:"metrics"`
46		Log       Log             `toml:"log"`
47		Scheduler SchedulerConfig `toml:"scheduler"`
48		FEC       FEC             `toml:"fec"`
49	}
50	
51	// SchedulerPolicy selects the send-side path-selection policy the multipath Bind
52	// runs. It is a bounded enum: the P1 active-backup failover (default) or the T21
53	// weighted-aggregation policy. The policy is an explicit config choice — active-
54	// backup stays selectable so the P1 behaviour is never removed, only extended.
55	type SchedulerPolicy string
56	
57	const (
58		// PolicyActiveBackup is the P1 single-active-path failover scheduler: all egress
59		// on the highest-priority live path, every other path idle (data-thrift by
60		// construction). It is the default when [scheduler] is omitted.
61		PolicyActiveBackup SchedulerPolicy = "active-backup"
62		// PolicyWeighted is the T21 weighted-aggregation scheduler: under load a single
63		// flow is striped across both paths in proportion to per-path RTT/loss-derived
64		// weight, collapsing to the primary at low load (5G stays ~idle) with hysteresis,
65		// and per-path send-pacing to bound bufferbloat.
66		PolicyWeighted SchedulerPolicy = "weighted"
67	)
68	
69	func (p SchedulerPolicy) valid() bool {
70		return p == PolicyActiveBackup || p == PolicyWeighted
71	}
72	
73	// Weighted-aggregation policy defaults (T21). They are applied when [scheduler]
74	// selects the weighted policy but leaves a knob at its zero value, so a minimal
75	// `policy = "weighted"` block is usable without hand-tuning every threshold. They
76	// are conservative bring-up values; the empirical per-path pace is sized from a
77	// measured BDP in a bandwidth-capped fixture (P0 findings §7, deferred to T35/T23),
78	// so the shipped default leaves pacing DISABLED and only bounds distribution.
79	const (
80		// defaultPerPathCapacityFPS is the reference per-path capacity, in frame-selection
81		// slots per second, that the aggregation load-gate compares offered load against
82		// AND (when pacing is enabled) the per-path token-bucket refill rate. It is a
83		// synthetic proxy for path capacity: there is no measured BDP (P0 §7), so capacity
84		// is expressed in the only unit the scheduler sees — Pick() invocations per second.
85		defaultPerPathCapacityFPS = 10000.0
86		// defaultEngageFraction engages aggregation once offered load exceeds this
87		// fraction of one path's capacity — i.e. a single path is nearly saturated.
88		defaultEngageFraction = 0.9
89		// defaultDisengageFraction collapses back to primary-only once offered load falls
90		// below this fraction of one path's capacity. It is strictly below the engage
91		// fraction: the gap is the hysteresis band that stops the 5G path from dribbling
92		// on/off around a single threshold (requirement 2, data-thrift).
93		defaultDisengageFraction = 0.5
94		// defaultCollapseDwell is how long offered load must stay continuously below the
95		// disengage fraction before aggregation collapses to primary-only — the temporal
96		// half of the hysteresis, mirroring the active-backup failback dwell so a brief
97		// lull does not repeatedly park then re-engage the metered path.
98		defaultCollapseDwell = 2 * time.Second
99		// defaultLoadTau is the time constant of the exponentially-weighted offered-load
100		// rate estimator. It smooths the instantaneous Pick() arrival rate so the gate
101		// reacts to sustained load, not single-frame bursts.
102		defaultLoadTau = 200 * time.Millisecond
103		// defaultPacingBurstFrames is the per-path token-bucket burst (in frame slots)
104		// when pacing is enabled: the bucket admits up to this many frames instantaneously
105		// before the refill rate binds, absorbing normal jitter without inflating a queue.
106		defaultPacingBurstFrames = 64.0
107		// defaultWeightRTTFloor floors the RTT in the weight formula so a path reporting a
108		// near-zero RTT (cold estimator, no samples yet) cannot be handed unbounded weight.
109		defaultWeightRTTFloor = 1 * time.Millisecond
110		// defaultWeightLossFloor floors the loss term under the square root so a zero-loss
111		// path gets a large-but-finite weight and two zero-loss paths split by inverse-RTT
112		// alone rather than both diverging.
113		defaultWeightLossFloor = 1e-3
114	)
115	
116	// SchedulerConfig selects and tunes the send scheduler. When the [scheduler] block
117	// is omitted the policy defaults to active-backup and every weighted knob is
118	// ignored, so existing configs keep the P1 behaviour unchanged. The weighted knobs
119	// are validated (and defaulted) only when the weighted policy is selected.
120	type SchedulerConfig struct {
121		// Policy selects the scheduler; defaults to active-backup when empty.
122		Policy SchedulerPolicy `toml:"policy"`
123	
124		// PerPathCapacityFPS is the reference per-path capacity in frame-selection slots
125		// per second: the denominator the aggregation load-gate compares offered load
126		// against, and the per-path pacing refill rate when PacingEnabled. Must be > 0
127		// under the weighted policy.
128		PerPathCapacityFPS float64 `toml:"per_path_capacity_fps"`
129		// EngageFraction engages aggregation when offered load exceeds
130		// EngageFraction*PerPathCapacityFPS. Must be in (0,1].
131		EngageFraction float64 `toml:"engage_fraction"`
132		// DisengageFraction collapses aggregation to primary-only once offered load stays
133		// below DisengageFraction*PerPathCapacityFPS for CollapseDwell. Must be in
134		// [0,EngageFraction) — strictly below EngageFraction so the two form a hysteresis
135		// band.
136		DisengageFraction float64 `toml:"disengage_fraction"`
137		// CollapseDwell is the sustained-low-load dwell before collapsing to primary-only
138		// (hysteresis). Must be >= 0.
139		CollapseDwell time.Duration `toml:"collapse_dwell"`
140		// LoadTau is the offered-load rate estimator's time constant. Must be > 0.
141		LoadTau time.Duration `toml:"load_tau"`
142	
143		// PacingEnabled turns per-path send-pacing on. When false the token buckets are
144		// bypassed (a documented no-op — P0 §7 could not empirically size the pace in the
145		// unmetered fixture), but PerPathCapacityFPS still drives the aggregation gate.
146		PacingEnabled bool `toml:"pacing_enabled"`
147		// PacingBurstFrames is the per-path token-bucket burst in frame slots. Must be > 0
148		// when PacingEnabled.
149		PacingBurstFrames float64 `toml:"pacing_burst_frames"`
150	
151		// WeightRTTFloor floors RTT in the weight formula (must be > 0 under weighted).
152		WeightRTTFloor time.Duration `toml:"weight_rtt_floor"`
153		// WeightLossFloor floors the loss term under the square root (must be > 0 under
154		// weighted).
155		WeightLossFloor float64 `toml:"weight_loss_floor"`
156	}
157	
158	// BDPSizing is a per-path pacing sizing derived from a MEASURED path, in the
159	// frame-selection-slot units the weighted scheduler pace uses (defect D22). It is the
160	// empirical alternative to the synthetic frame-count defaults: CapacityFPS replaces
161	// PerPathCapacityFPS and BurstFrames replaces PacingBurstFrames.
162	type BDPSizing struct {
163		// CapacityFPS is the sustained per-path frame rate the measured bottleneck bandwidth
164		// supports (the token-bucket refill rate and aggregation-gate denominator).
165		CapacityFPS float64
166		// BurstFrames is the bandwidth-delay product expressed in frames (bandwidth x RTT,
167		// i.e. one RTT of in-flight frames): the token-bucket burst that absorbs one RTT of
168		// jitter without building a standing queue.
169		BurstFrames float64
170	}
171	
172	// defaultAvgWireFrameBytes is the conservative average on-wire outer-frame size used
173	// when deriving the per-path pace from an operator-declared bandwidth at config load
174	// (T53): a full IPv4 path MTU, since a full-MTU DATA datagram occupies about one path
175	// MTU on the wire. It mirrors bind.DefaultPathMTU (1500) rather than importing it —
176	// internal/bind imports internal/config, so config cannot import bind without a cycle
177	// (the same mirror-with-cross-reference pattern as maxFECDeadline above). Sizing
178	// capacity with the full-MTU frame is the conservative floor: smaller average frames
179	// would yield a HIGHER frame rate, so this never over-paces a path.
180	const defaultAvgWireFrameBytes = 1500.0
181	
182	// SizePacingFromBDP derives the weighted scheduler's per-path pacing parameters from a
183	// measured path instead of the synthetic frame-count default (defect D22). The shipped
184	// defaultPerPathCapacityFPS (10000, ~115 Mbit/s at full MTU) sits far above a realistic
185	// slow uplink, so the aggregation gate may never engage and — with pacing enabled — the
186	// pace never binds; deriving capacity from the measured bottleneck bandwidth fixes both.
187	//
188	// capacity_fps = bandwidth / (8 * avg wire frame bytes) — frames/s the link sustains.
189	// burst_frames = capacity_fps * RTT — one RTT of in-flight frames (equivalently
190	// BDP_bytes / avg wire frame bytes, since the bandwidth-delay product BDP = bandwidth * RTT).
191	//
192	// bandwidthBitsPerSec is the measured bottleneck bandwidth (bits/s), rtt the measured
193	// path RTT, and avgWireFrameBytes the average on-wire outer-frame size (a full-MTU
194	// datagram plus frame.DataOverhead is the conservative choice). It fails fast on a
195	// non-positive input rather than emitting a nonsensical (zero/negative/Inf) sizing.
196	func SizePacingFromBDP(bandwidthBitsPerSec float64, rtt time.Duration, avgWireFrameBytes float64) (BDPSizing, error) {
197		if bandwidthBitsPerSec <= 0 {
198			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing bandwidth must be > 0 bit/s, got %g", bandwidthBitsPerSec)
199		}
200		if rtt <= 0 {
201			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing RTT must be > 0, got %s", rtt)
202		}
203		if avgWireFrameBytes <= 0 {
204			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing average wire frame size must be > 0 bytes, got %g", avgWireFrameBytes)
205		}
206		const bitsPerByte = 8.0
207		capacityFPS := bandwidthBitsPerSec / (bitsPerByte * avgWireFrameBytes)
208		burstFrames := capacityFPS * rtt.Seconds()
209		return BDPSizing{CapacityFPS: capacityFPS, BurstFrames: burstFrames}, nil
210	}

212	// bandwidthUnit is a recognised link_bandwidth suffix and its bit/s multiplier.
213	type bandwidthUnit struct {
214		suffix string
215		mult   float64
216	}
217	
218	// bandwidthUnits are the accepted operator-facing bandwidth suffixes, longest-first so
219	// "gbit" is matched before "bit". SI decimal multipliers (k/M/G = 1e3/1e6/1e9) over a
220	// bit/s base; "bps" is accepted as an alias of "bit" (and "kbps"/"mbps"/"gbps" likewise).
221	var bandwidthUnits = []bandwidthUnit{
222		{"gbit", 1e9}, {"gbps", 1e9},
223		{"mbit", 1e6}, {"mbps", 1e6},
224		{"kbit", 1e3}, {"kbps", 1e3},
225		{"bit", 1}, {"bps", 1},
226	}
227	
228	// parseBandwidth parses an operator-declared link bandwidth such as "50Mbit", "1Gbit",
229	// or "500kbit" into bits per second. It requires an explicit bit/s unit suffix so a
230	// bare unitless number cannot be silently misread, and fails fast on an empty or
231	// otherwise unparseable value — a mistyped bandwidth is rejected at config load.
232	func parseBandwidth(s string) (float64, error) {
233		lower := strings.ToLower(strings.TrimSpace(s))
234		if lower == "" {
235			return 0, errors.New("empty bandwidth")
236		}
237		for _, u := range bandwidthUnits {
238			if !strings.HasSuffix(lower, u.suffix) {
239				continue
240			}
241			num := strings.TrimSpace(lower[:len(lower)-len(u.suffix)])
242			val, err := strconv.ParseFloat(num, 64)
243			if err != nil {
244				return 0, fmt.Errorf("invalid number %q", num)
245			}
246			return val * u.mult, nil
247		}
248		return 0, fmt.Errorf("missing bit/s unit suffix (want e.g. %q)", "50Mbit")
249	}

251	// FEC group-close deadline default (T24). Applied only when [fec] is enabled but
252	// leaves the deadline at its zero value, so a minimal `enabled = true` block emits
253	// parity for a partially-filled group promptly under low load rather than stranding
254	// it until the size threshold fills.
255	const defaultFECDeadline = 5 * time.Millisecond
256	
257	// defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor mirrors adaptivefec.DefaultSafetyFactor (1.5), the
258	// simulation-proven controller default, restated here (with this cross-reference rather
259	// than an import) so a minimal `adaptive = true` block gets the proven tuning without
260	// coupling config to the controller package. NOTE: 1.5 sizes M for the mean loss with
261	// modest variance headroom; a tight residual SLA under a specific loss/geometry may need a
262	// higher factor (e.g. at 5% loss with K=10 the 1.5 default sizes M=1, ~1% residual — raise
263	// this to lift M and drive the residual down).
264	const defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor = 1.5
265	
266	// maxFECDeadline bounds the FEC group-close deadline at load time (T24, defect #4). It
267	// MUST stay at or below the multipath Bind's authoritative bound (bind.maxFECDeadline =
268	// resequencerTimeout/2 = 125ms): a group flushed by the deadline emits its parity
269	// `deadline` after opening, and the reconstructed frames must reach the receive
270	// resequencer BEFORE it skips the gap (its 250ms per-gap timeout) — otherwise recovery
271	// is structurally too late (the gap is skipped, the recovered frame dropped as past the
272	// release point) while /metrics would still count it reconstructed. Rejecting an
273	// over-large deadline at load makes that coupling fail-fast and explicit rather than
274	// silently defeating FEC. Kept in lockstep with bind.maxFECDeadline (the packages
275	// cannot import each other, so the value is mirrored with this cross-reference).
276	const maxFECDeadline = 125 * time.Millisecond
277	
278	// maxFECShards mirrors the Reed-Solomon field limit enforced in internal/fec: a
279	// coding group carries at most 256 shards (data + parity) total over GF(2^8). It
280	// is restated here so config load fails fast on an over-large ratio at the right
281	// locus, with the same bound the codec enforces internally.
282	const maxFECShards = 256
283	
284	// FEC configures the fixed-ratio Reed-Solomon forward-error-correction plane (T24).
285	// It is DISABLED by default (like pacing), so an existing config with no [fec] block
286	// runs the datapath exactly as before — FEC transparent, no parity on the wire. When
287	// enabled, each group of DataShards (K) inner datagrams is protected by ParityShards
288	// (M) parity frames, letting the receiver reconstruct up to M lost data frames per
289	// group at a fixed M/K parity overhead.
290	type FEC struct {
291		// Enabled turns the FEC plane on. When false every other field is ignored and the
292		// datapath carries no parity.
293		Enabled bool `toml:"enabled"`
294		// DataShards is K: the number of inner datagrams grouped before parity is emitted.
295		// Must be >= 1 when enabled.
296		DataShards int `toml:"data_shards"`
297		// ParityShards is M: the parity frames emitted per group and the maximum number of
298		// per-group data losses the receiver can recover. Must be >= 1 when enabled. In
299		// adaptive mode it is the CEILING (and the receiver's decoder cardinality) — the
300		// controller drives the per-group parity in [0,ParityShards] to track measured loss.
301		ParityShards int `toml:"parity_shards"`
302		// Deadline bounds grouping latency: a partially-filled group is flushed (parity
303		// emitted over its current data frames) once this much time has elapsed since its
304		// first frame. Defaults to defaultFECDeadline when enabled and left zero; must be
305		// > 0 after defaulting.
306		Deadline time.Duration `toml:"deadline"`
307		// Adaptive opts the send-side FEC into the closed-loop controller (T27/T29): the
308		// per-group parity count tracks the measured per-path loss instead of standing at
309		// the fixed ParityShards ratio, so a clean path spends near-zero overhead while a
310		// lossy one is masked. It is OFF by default, so an existing [fec] block keeps the
311		// fixed-ratio behaviour (T24) byte-for-byte. When true, ParityShards is reinterpreted
312		// as the controller's parity ceiling (see above); the receiver is unchanged because a
313		// group coded with fewer parity shards decodes against the ParityShards-ceiling codec
314		// unchanged (klauspost's parity is prefix-consistent).
315		Adaptive bool `toml:"adaptive"`
316		// SafetyFactor is the adaptive controller's headroom multiplier over the measured mean
317		// loss the per-group parity is sized to mask (adaptivefec.Config.SafetyFactor). It is
318		// the residual-loss LEVER: the controller sizes M so M/(K+M) >= SafetyFactor*loss, so a
319		// higher factor spends more parity to keep the post-recovery residual under a tighter
320		// bound against binomial per-group variance. Applies only in adaptive mode; defaults to
321		// defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor (the simulation-proven controller default) when left zero
322		// AND target_residual is unset, and must be >= 1. Ignored (and must stay zero) in fixed
323		// mode, and mutually exclusive with target_residual (set one, not both).
324		SafetyFactor float64 `toml:"safety_factor"`
325		// TargetResidual is the adaptive controller's target POST-RECOVERY residual-loss SLA
326		// (a fraction in (0,1)) and the PRIMARY adaptive sizing surface (D26/T46): the
327		// controller derives the per-group parity M by inverting the binomial residual model
328		// E[max(0,D-M)]/K (D ~ Bin(K, smoothed loss)) to the smallest M meeting this target,
329		// capped at the parity ceiling (parity_shards). It SUPERSEDES safety_factor — set
330		// EITHER a residual SLA (target_residual, recommended: it maps an operator's loss
331		// budget directly to redundancy) OR the bare headroom multiplier (safety_factor), never
332		// both. Applies only in adaptive mode; unset (0) selects the safety_factor path. Must
333		// be in (0,1) when set. Ignored (and must stay zero) in fixed mode.
334		TargetResidual float64 `toml:"target_residual"`
335	}
336	
337	// applyDefaults fills the group-close deadline when FEC is enabled and the deadline
338	// was left at zero. It is a no-op for a disabled block, so a config that never turns
339	// FEC on keeps an empty [fec] surface.
340	func (f *FEC) applyDefaults() {
341		if !f.Enabled {
342			return
343		}
344		if f.Deadline == 0 {
345			f.Deadline = defaultFECDeadline
346		}
347		// The SafetyFactor default is applied only in the legacy sizing mode: when
348		// target_residual is set it is the primary surface and safety_factor stays inert
349		// (0), so defaulting it would spuriously trip the mutual-exclusion check.
350		if f.Adaptive && f.TargetResidual == 0 && f.SafetyFactor == 0 {
351			f.SafetyFactor = defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor
352		}
353	}
354	
355	// validate enforces the FEC ratio invariants, failing fast so a mis-tuned parity
356	// ratio is rejected at load rather than panicking the codec at runtime. A disabled
357	// block needs no tuning. The bounds mirror internal/fec's own Config.validate.
358	func (f FEC) validate() error {
359		if !f.Enabled {
360			if f.Adaptive {
361				return errors.New("fec.adaptive = true requires fec.enabled = true (adaptive FEC is meaningless with the plane off)")
362			}
363			return nil
364		}
365		if f.DataShards < 1 {
366			return fmt.Errorf("fec.data_shards must be >= 1 when FEC is enabled, got %d", f.DataShards)
367		}
368		if f.ParityShards < 1 {
369			return fmt.Errorf("fec.parity_shards must be >= 1 when FEC is enabled, got %d", f.ParityShards)
370		}
371		if f.DataShards+f.ParityShards > maxFECShards {
372			return fmt.Errorf("fec.data_shards + fec.parity_shards must be <= %d (Reed-Solomon field limit), got %d", maxFECShards, f.DataShards+f.ParityShards)
373		}
374		if f.Deadline <= 0 {
375			return fmt.Errorf("fec.deadline must be > 0 when FEC is enabled, got %s", f.Deadline)
376		}
377		if f.Deadline > maxFECDeadline {
378			return fmt.Errorf("fec.deadline must be <= %s (safely below the receive resequencer's per-gap timeout so deadline-flushed recovery lands before the gap is skipped), got %s", maxFECDeadline, f.Deadline)
379		}
380		if f.Adaptive {
381			if f.TargetResidual != 0 {
382				// Residual-SLA sizing mode (primary): target_residual governs and safety_factor
383				// stays inert, so setting both is an ambiguous mis-config — reject it. NaN is
384				// caught explicitly because every ordered comparison against it is false.
385				if math.IsNaN(f.TargetResidual) || f.TargetResidual <= 0 || f.TargetResidual >= 1 {
386					return fmt.Errorf("fec.target_residual must be a finite value in (0,1), got %g", f.TargetResidual)
387				}
388				if f.SafetyFactor != 0 {
389					return fmt.Errorf("fec.safety_factor and fec.target_residual are mutually exclusive; target_residual is the primary residual-SLA surface — set one, not both")
390				}
391			} else if f.SafetyFactor < 1 {
392				// SafetyFactor is defaulted to defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor when zero, so a value
393				// below 1 here is an explicit mis-set: the controller requires >= 1 (masking less
394				// than the mean loss is nonsensical), matching adaptivefec.Config.Validate.
395				return fmt.Errorf("fec.safety_factor must be >= 1 in adaptive mode, got %g", f.SafetyFactor)
396			}
397		} else if f.SafetyFactor != 0 || f.TargetResidual != 0 {
398			return fmt.Errorf("fec.safety_factor / fec.target_residual are only meaningful in adaptive mode (set fec.adaptive = true), got safety_factor=%g target_residual=%g", f.SafetyFactor, f.TargetResidual)
399		}
400		return nil
401	}

403	// Path is one physical WAN uplink. The edge binds each path's UDP socket to
404	// SourceAddr so the upstream router pins it to the intended WAN; the
405	// concentrator learns the real per-path endpoints from authenticated traffic.
406	type Path struct {
407		// Name is a stable human-readable identifier for the path (e.g. "starlink").
408		Name string `toml:"name"`
409		// SourceAddr is the local source IP the path's socket binds to.
410		SourceAddr netip.Addr `toml:"-"`
411		// SourceAddrRaw is the TOML string form of SourceAddr; parsed in normalize.
412		SourceAddrRaw string `toml:"source_addr"`
413		// DestAddr is the OPTIONAL per-path concentrator endpoint ("ip:port") the
414		// edge sends this path's datagrams to. It is unset on the concentrator (which
415		// learns edge endpoints from traffic) and optional on the edge: when a single
416		// public concentrator IP fronts all uplinks (the common source-routed case)
417		// the peer's wireguard endpoint is reused for every path; when the paths reach
418		// the concentrator on distinct addresses, set dest_addr per path.
419		DestAddr netip.AddrPort `toml:"-"`
420		// DestAddrRaw is the TOML string form of DestAddr; parsed in normalize.
421		DestAddrRaw string `toml:"dest_addr"`
422		// LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec is the OPERATOR-DECLARED bottleneck bandwidth of this
423		// uplink in bits/s, parsed from LinkBandwidthRaw in normalize. It is used ONLY to
424		// size the weighted scheduler's per-path pace from the bandwidth-delay product
425		// (SizePacingFromBDP) when the weighted policy runs with pacing ENABLED (T53, Q20);
426		// it is OPERATOR-DECLARED, not runtime-measured — wanbond never auto-tunes it live.
427		// Zero means "not declared" (the synthetic default pace is kept).
428		LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec float64 `toml:"-"`
429		// LinkBandwidthRaw is the TOML string form of the declared bandwidth, e.g.
430		// "50Mbit" / "1Gbit" / "500kbit" (SI bit/s units; the "bit" suffix may be written
431		// "bps"). Parsed in normalize; a non-positive or unparseable value fails fast.
432		LinkBandwidthRaw string `toml:"link_bandwidth"`
433		// LinkRTT is the OPERATOR-DECLARED baseline RTT of this uplink, parsed from
434		// LinkRTTRaw in normalize. It is the delay term of the bandwidth-delay-product pace
435		// burst (one RTT of in-flight frames); required (> 0) when LinkBandwidth is set and
436		// pacing is enabled under the weighted policy, ignored otherwise.
437		LinkRTT time.Duration `toml:"-"`
438		// LinkRTTRaw is the TOML Go-duration string form of LinkRTT, e.g. "45ms". Parsed in
439		// normalize; an unparseable or non-positive value fails fast.
440		LinkRTTRaw string `toml:"link_rtt"`
441	}

443	// WireGuard holds the inner tunnel's key material.
444	type WireGuard struct {
445		PrivateKey Key    `toml:"private_key"`
446		Peers      []Peer `toml:"peers"`
447		// ListenPort is the UDP port the concentrator listens on; 0 on the edge.
448		ListenPort uint16 `toml:"listen_port"`
449	}

451	// Peer is one WireGuard peer.
452	type Peer struct {
453		PublicKey Key `toml:"public_key"`
454		// Endpoint is the peer's SINGLE tunnel address (edge -> concentrator); the
455		// legacy single-endpoint config form, mutually exclusive with EndpointsRaw.
456		// Empty on the concentrator, which roams the edge's endpoint dynamically.
457		// A bare `endpoint = "..."` is normalized in resolveEndpoints to a one-
458		// element Endpoints list, so it stays behavior-identical to the pre-T54
459		// config surface.
460		Endpoint string `toml:"endpoint"`
461		// EndpointsRaw is the ORDERED list of concentrator (peer) endpoint address:port
462		// strings for the edge-side hub-failover config surface (Q18): index 0 is the
463		// active/primary concentrator, the rest are ordered standbys. Edge-only; a
464		// concentrator declaring it is a config error (validate). Mutually exclusive
465		// with the legacy Endpoint field.
466		EndpointsRaw []string `toml:"endpoints"`
467		// Endpoints is EndpointsRaw (or the single legacy Endpoint, as a one-element
468		// list) parsed to netip.AddrPort and de-duplicated; populated by
469		// resolveEndpoints in normalize(). This is the shape T57's hub-failover
470		// switch consumes: Endpoints[0] is the active concentrator, Endpoints[1:]
471		// are the ordered standbys to fail over to in order when all paths to the
472		// active one are down. A hostname entry (Q35) contributes NO element here at
473		// load time — resolution happens at runtime, not at config load (Q30) — so
474		// Endpoints only ever holds the literal-address entries, in order.
475		Endpoints []netip.AddrPort `toml:"-"`
476		// DNS is the explicit per-peer opt-in (Q29) for hostname endpoint entries: a
477		// hostname entry in endpoint/endpoints without dns = true is a config load
478		// error naming this flag. Default-off, so an existing IP-literal config never
479		// takes a new code path. Edge-only; a concentrator declaring dns = true is a
480		// config error, mirroring the endpoints-not-meaningful-for-concentrator rule.
481		DNS bool `toml:"dns"`
482		// EndpointSpecs is the ordered, typed per-entry parse of the endpoint/
483		// endpoints entries (Q35): each entry is either a literal address:port
484		// (IsName=false, Addr set — parsed with EXACTLY today's netip.ParseAddrPort
485		// path) or, when DNS opts in, a hostname:port (IsName=true, Host/Port set).
486		// Populated by resolveEndpoints in normalize(); order matches the TOML list.
487		EndpointSpecs []EndpointSpec `toml:"-"`
488		// AllowedIPs are the CIDR ranges routed to this peer.
489		AllowedIPs []string `toml:"allowed_ips"`
490		// PSK is this peer's per-peer override of the outer-control PSK (G4
491		// multi-peer concentrator groundwork). With a single configured peer it
492		// must be left UNSET: the top-level Config.PSK remains the single-peer
493		// default, so a legacy single-peer config carrying only the top-level
494		// `psk` parses and behaves byte-identically to before this field existed.
495		// With more than one peer, validate requires it to be present and
496		// pairwise-distinct across peers (T81, Q21) — the top-level psk alone
497		// cannot discriminate which peer authenticated an inbound frame, and equal
498		// per-peer psks would defeat that authenticated demux. No datapath code
499		// path consumes PSK yet; it is parsed, validated, and exposed only.
500		PSK Key `toml:"psk"`
501		// Name is this peer's human-readable identifier (G4 multi-peer
502		// concentrator groundwork), analogous to Path.Name. Unused and optional
503		// with a single peer; required and must be unique across peers when more
504		// than one is configured (T81, Q21). Not yet consumed by any datapath code
505		// path.
506		Name string `toml:"name"`
507	}

509	// EndpointSpec is one parsed peer endpoint entry (Q35). A literal address:port
510	// entry parses via netip.ParseAddrPort and sets Addr (IsName=false); a
511	// hostname:port entry (accepted only when the owning Peer opts in with
512	// dns = true) sets Host/Port and IsName=true instead — no resolution happens at
513	// config load (Q30 defers it to runtime).
514	type EndpointSpec struct {
515		// Host is the hostname, set only when IsName is true.
516		Host string
517		// Port is the port for a hostname entry, set only when IsName is true.
518		Port uint16
519		// Addr is the parsed literal address:port, set only when IsName is false.
520		Addr netip.AddrPort
521		// IsName reports whether this entry is a hostname (true) or an IP literal
522		// (false).
523		IsName bool
524	}
525	
526	// resolveEndpoints canonicalizes the peer's endpoint config into the ordered
527	// Endpoints list (Q18) and the ordered, typed EndpointSpecs list (Q35): the
528	// legacy single Endpoint field and the new ordered EndpointsRaw list are
529	// mutually exclusive input forms, but a bare `endpoint = "..."` is normalized
530	// here to a ONE-ELEMENT list — so it stays behavior-identical to the pre-T54
531	// single-defaultRemote config surface. Each entry is tried FIRST as a literal
532	// netip.AddrPort ("host:port"), the same format bind.Multipath.ParseEndpoint
533	// requires of the UAPI endpoint string at runtime — an all-literal config
534	// takes EXACTLY this path, unchanged, with the same errors and the same
535	// duplicate detection as before T67 (Q29). Only when that parse fails, AND the
536	// host portion is not itself a malformed IP literal, is the entry split as
537	// host:port and treated as a hostname, gated behind the peer's explicit DNS
538	// opt-in (Q29); a hostname entry without the opt-in is a config error naming
539	// the flag. No resolution happens here (Q30 defers it to runtime). Duplicates
540	// are rejected within each of the two namespaces: a literal duplicating a
541	// literal, or a hostname:port duplicating another hostname:port.
542	func (p *Peer) resolveEndpoints() error {
543		if p.Endpoint != "" && len(p.EndpointsRaw) > 0 {
544			return errors.New("endpoint and endpoints are mutually exclusive; endpoint is the single-entry legacy form of endpoints")
545		}
546		raw := p.EndpointsRaw
547		if p.Endpoint != "" {
548			raw = []string{p.Endpoint}
549		}
550		seen := make(map[netip.AddrPort]struct{}, len(raw))
551		seenNames := make(map[string]struct{}, len(raw))
552		endpoints := make([]netip.AddrPort, 0, len(raw))
553		specs := make([]EndpointSpec, 0, len(raw))
554		for _, s := range raw {
555			ap, err := netip.ParseAddrPort(s)
556			if err == nil {
557				if _, dup := seen[ap]; dup {
558					return fmt.Errorf("duplicate endpoint %q", s)
559				}
560				seen[ap] = struct{}{}
561				endpoints = append(endpoints, ap)
562				specs = append(specs, EndpointSpec{Addr: ap})
563				continue
564			}
565			host, portStr, splitErr := net.SplitHostPort(s)
566			if splitErr != nil {
567				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: %w", s, err)
568			}
569			// The host portion parses as an IP: this is a malformed IP-literal entry
570			// (e.g. a bad port), not a hostname — report the ORIGINAL ParseAddrPort
571			// error rather than diverting it into the hostname path, so every
572			// IP-shaped entry keeps today's exact error.
573			if _, ipErr := netip.ParseAddr(host); ipErr == nil {
574				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: %w", s, err)
575			}
576			if !p.DNS {
577				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: hostname endpoints require the peer's dns = true opt-in flag", s)
578			}
579			port, portErr := strconv.ParseUint(portStr, 10, 16)
580			if portErr != nil || port == 0 {
581				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: invalid port %q", s, portStr)
582			}
583			if hostErr := validateHostname(host); hostErr != nil {
584				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: %w", s, hostErr)
585			}
586			nameKey := host + ":" + portStr
587			if _, dup := seenNames[nameKey]; dup {
588				return fmt.Errorf("duplicate endpoint %q", s)
589			}
590			seenNames[nameKey] = struct{}{}
591			specs = append(specs, EndpointSpec{Host: host, Port: uint16(port), IsName: true})
592		}
593		p.Endpoints = endpoints
594		p.EndpointSpecs = specs
595		return nil
596	}
597	
598	// validateHostname reports whether host is a syntactically valid DNS hostname
599	// (RFC 1123 label rules): 1-253 characters total, each dot-separated label
600	// 1-63 characters of letters/digits/hyphens, no leading or trailing hyphen.
601	func validateHostname(host string) error {
602		if len(host) == 0 || len(host) > 253 {
603			return fmt.Errorf("hostname %q must be 1-253 characters", host)
604		}
605		for _, label := range strings.Split(host, ".") {
606			if len(label) == 0 || len(label) > 63 {
607				return fmt.Errorf("hostname label %q must be 1-63 characters", label)
608			}
609			if label[0] == '-' || label[len(label)-1] == '-' {
610				return fmt.Errorf("hostname label %q must not start or end with a hyphen", label)
611			}
612			for _, r := range label {
613				alnum := (r >= 'a' && r <= 'z') || (r >= 'A' && r <= 'Z') || (r >= '0' && r <= '9')
614				if !alnum && r != '-' {
615					return fmt.Errorf("hostname label %q contains invalid character %q", label, r)
616				}
617			}
618		}
619		return nil
620	}

622	// PeerIdentity is one configured WireGuard peer's effective outer-control PSK
623	// and stable name/id (G4 multi-peer concentrator groundwork).
624	type PeerIdentity struct {
625		// PSK is this peer's effective outer-control PSK: for a single-peer config
626		// it is the top-level Config.PSK (the pre-G4 back-compat default, unaffected
627		// by any per-peer psk); for a multi-peer config it is the peer's own PSK
628		// field. validate() rejects a per-peer psk when exactly one peer is
629		// configured, so the single-peer-with-distinct-per-peer-psk shape is not
630		// loadable via config.Load; this field still defends the invariant
631		// defensively for any Config value built directly.
632		PSK Key
633		// Name is this peer's stable identifier: its configured Name when set,
634		// otherwise a fallback derived from its public key (the first 8 bytes,
635		// lowercase hex — the same short-key form uapiConfig uses to name a peer
636		// in error messages), so every peer has a non-empty, stable id even when
637		// name is omitted.
638		Name string
639	}
640	
641	// PeerIdentities returns, for each configured WireGuard peer in order, its
642	// effective PSK and stable name/id (G4). This is the SINGLE place the
643	// single-peer/multi-peer PSK back-compat decision is made: device.Up and the
644	// Bind consume PeerIdentities instead of each re-deriving the effective PSK
645	// from Config.PSK vs Peer.PSK. Order matches c.WireGuard.Peers.
646	func (c Config) PeerIdentities() []PeerIdentity {
647		peers := c.WireGuard.Peers
648		ids := make([]PeerIdentity, len(peers))
649		for i, p := range peers {
650			psk := p.PSK
651			if len(peers) == 1 {
652				// Single-peer back-compat: the top-level psk remains the effective
653				// PSK regardless of whether a per-peer psk is also set.
654				psk = c.PSK
655			}
656			name := p.Name
657			if name == "" {
658				pub := p.PublicKey.Bytes()
659				name = hex.EncodeToString(pub[:8])
660			}
661			ids[i] = PeerIdentity{PSK: psk, Name: name}
662		}
663		return ids
664	}

666	// Amnezia holds the amneziawg-go obfuscation parameters. They must match on both
667	// ends for the handshake to succeed; they are defense-in-depth only.
668	//
669	// The block is "all-or-nothing": either every junk/size knob is left zero (plain
670	// WireGuard) or the whole obfuscation set (jc, jmin, jmax, s1, s2) is specified.
671	// A PARTIAL block silently produces an obfuscation profile the two ends cannot
672	// agree on, so validate rejects it at load (defect D1). The four magic headers
673	// (h1-h4) default to the standard message-type values 1..4 when omitted.
674	type Amnezia struct {
675		Jc   int    `toml:"jc"`
676		Jmin int    `toml:"jmin"`
677		Jmax int    `toml:"jmax"`
678		S1   int    `toml:"s1"`
679		S2   int    `toml:"s2"`
680		H1   uint32 `toml:"h1"`
681		H2   uint32 `toml:"h2"`
682		H3   uint32 `toml:"h3"`
683		H4   uint32 `toml:"h4"`
684	}
685	
686	// defaultMagicHeaders are the standard WireGuard message-type headers: initiation,
687	// response, cookie-reply, and transport. amneziawg-go treats any magic header <= 4
688	// as "use the standard type", so 1..4 is the canonical "headers not obfuscated"
689	// profile. wanbond emits them explicitly (rather than 0) so a configured amnezia
690	// block always carries a complete, self-consistent set of magic headers.
691	var defaultMagicHeaders = [4]uint32{1, 2, 3, 4}
692	
693	// Configured reports whether the amnezia block carries any obfuscation parameter.
694	// An all-zero block leaves the engine in plain WireGuard mode.
695	func (a Amnezia) Configured() bool {
696		return a.Jc != 0 || a.Jmin != 0 || a.Jmax != 0 || a.S1 != 0 || a.S2 != 0 ||
697			a.H1 != 0 || a.H2 != 0 || a.H3 != 0 || a.H4 != 0
698	}
699	
700	// applyDefaults fills in the standard magic headers (1..4) when the block is
701	// configured but no header was given, so the UAPI renderer emits an explicit,
702	// complete header set instead of h1=0..h4=0. It is a no-op for an unconfigured
703	// block and for one that already sets any header (a partial header set is left
704	// intact so validate can reject it).
705	func (a *Amnezia) applyDefaults() {
706		if !a.Configured() {
707			return
708		}
709		if a.H1 == 0 && a.H2 == 0 && a.H3 == 0 && a.H4 == 0 {
710			a.H1, a.H2, a.H3, a.H4 = defaultMagicHeaders[0], defaultMagicHeaders[1], defaultMagicHeaders[2], defaultMagicHeaders[3]
711		}
712	}

725	// Key is a 32-byte Curve25519 key or PSK, carried in TOML as standard base64.
726	type Key struct {
727		bytes [keyLen]byte
728		set   bool
729	}
730	
731	// UnmarshalText decodes a base64 key. An empty string leaves the Key unset so
732	// optional keys can be distinguished from present-but-invalid ones.
733	func (k *Key) UnmarshalText(text []byte) error {
734		if len(text) == 0 {
735			return nil
736		}
737		raw, err := base64.StdEncoding.DecodeString(string(text))
738		if err != nil {
739			return fmt.Errorf("key is not valid base64: %w", err)
740		}
741		if len(raw) != keyLen {
742			return fmt.Errorf("key must decode to %d bytes, got %d", keyLen, len(raw))
743		}
744		copy(k.bytes[:], raw)
745		k.set = true
746		return nil
747	}
748	
749	// IsSet reports whether the key was present and valid.
750	func (k Key) IsSet() bool { return k.set }
751	
752	// Bytes returns the raw key material.
753	func (k Key) Bytes() [keyLen]byte { return k.bytes }

755	// normalize parses the string-typed fields (addresses) into their typed forms.
756	func (c *Config) normalize() error {
757		for i := range c.Paths {
758			p := &c.Paths[i]
759			if p.SourceAddrRaw != "" {
760				addr, err := netip.ParseAddr(p.SourceAddrRaw)
761				if err != nil {
762					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: invalid source_addr %q: %w", p.Name, p.SourceAddrRaw, err)
763				}
764				p.SourceAddr = addr
765			}
766			if p.DestAddrRaw != "" {
767				dst, err := netip.ParseAddrPort(p.DestAddrRaw)
768				if err != nil {
769					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: invalid dest_addr %q: %w", p.Name, p.DestAddrRaw, err)
770				}
771				p.DestAddr = dst
772			}
773			if p.LinkBandwidthRaw != "" {
774				bw, err := parseBandwidth(p.LinkBandwidthRaw)
775				if err != nil {
776					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: invalid link_bandwidth %q: %w", p.Name, p.LinkBandwidthRaw, err)
777				}
778				if bw <= 0 {
779					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: link_bandwidth must be > 0, got %q", p.Name, p.LinkBandwidthRaw)
780				}
781				p.LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec = bw
782			}
783			if p.LinkRTTRaw != "" {
784				rtt, err := time.ParseDuration(p.LinkRTTRaw)
785				if err != nil {
786					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: invalid link_rtt %q: %w", p.Name, p.LinkRTTRaw, err)
787				}
788				if rtt <= 0 {
789					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: link_rtt must be > 0, got %q", p.Name, p.LinkRTTRaw)
790				}
791				p.LinkRTT = rtt
792			}
793		}
794		for i := range c.WireGuard.Peers {
795			if err := c.WireGuard.Peers[i].resolveEndpoints(); err != nil {
796				return fmt.Errorf("wireguard peer %d: %w", i, err)
797			}
798		}
799		c.Amnezia.applyDefaults()
800		// Derive the weighted pace from any operator-declared per-link bandwidth BEFORE
801		// applyDefaults, so it can (a) see the raw zero PerPathCapacityFPS to distinguish an
802		// explicit knob from the default and (b) set the derived capacity/burst that
803		// applyDefaults then leaves intact (it only fills a knob left at zero). Under a
804		// disabled or non-weighted pacing it is a no-op, so the synthetic default is kept.
805		if err := c.deriveWeightedPacingFromBDP(); err != nil {
806			return err
807		}
808		c.Scheduler.applyDefaults()
809		c.FEC.applyDefaults()
810		return nil
811	}
812	
813	// deriveWeightedPacingFromBDP sizes the weighted scheduler's per-path pace from the
814	// operator-declared per-link bandwidth (T53, Q20) via SizePacingFromBDP instead of the
815	// synthetic defaultPerPathCapacityFPS. It runs only under the weighted policy with
816	// pacing ENABLED and at least one declared link_bandwidth; otherwise a declared
817	// bandwidth is inert (pacing ships DISABLED by default), so an unrelated config is
818	// untouched. The value is OPERATOR-DECLARED and fixed at load — NOT runtime auto-tuning
819	// (Q20 rejected a live control loop for the pilot).
820	//
821	// The scheduler carries a SINGLE reference per-path capacity applied to every path's
822	// token bucket, so a heterogeneous link set is sized to the BOTTLENECK: the slowest
823	// declared link governs the shared pace, because pacing any path faster than the
824	// slowest link's capacity would let that link build the very standing queue pacing
825	// exists to prevent. All paths must therefore declare a bandwidth (all-or-nothing) so
826	// the bottleneck is well-defined across the whole path set.
827	func (c *Config) deriveWeightedPacingFromBDP() error {
828		s := &c.Scheduler
829		if s.Policy != PolicyWeighted || !s.PacingEnabled {
830			return nil
831		}
832		declared := 0
833		for i := range c.Paths {
834			if c.Paths[i].LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec > 0 {
835				declared++
836			}
837		}
838		if declared == 0 {
839			return nil // no declared bandwidth: the synthetic default pace is preserved.
840		}
841		if declared != len(c.Paths) {
842			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler pacing: link_bandwidth must be declared on ALL paths or none (got %d of %d) — the shared per-path pace is sized to the slowest declared link, which is undefined with a partial declaration", declared, len(c.Paths))
843		}
844		if s.PerPathCapacityFPS != 0 || s.PacingBurstFrames != 0 {
845			return errors.New("scheduler.per_path_capacity_fps / pacing_burst_frames and per-path link_bandwidth are mutually exclusive: declare the link bandwidth (BDP-derived pace) OR set the raw frame-slot knobs, not both")
846		}
847		var bottleneck BDPSizing
848		for i := range c.Paths {
849			p := &c.Paths[i]
850			if p.LinkRTT <= 0 {
851				return fmt.Errorf("path %q: link_rtt is required (> 0) when link_bandwidth is set under weighted pacing — it is the delay term of the bandwidth-delay-product burst", p.Name)
852			}
853			sz, err := SizePacingFromBDP(p.LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec, p.LinkRTT, defaultAvgWireFrameBytes)
854			if err != nil {
855				return fmt.Errorf("path %q: %w", p.Name, err)
856			}
857			if i == 0 || sz.CapacityFPS < bottleneck.CapacityFPS {
858				bottleneck = sz
859			}
860		}
861		s.PerPathCapacityFPS = bottleneck.CapacityFPS
862		s.PacingBurstFrames = bottleneck.BurstFrames
863		return nil
864	}
865	
866	// applyDefaults selects active-backup when no policy is given and, only under the
867	// weighted policy, fills any weighted knob left at its zero value with its default.
868	// It is a no-op for the active-backup policy: those weighted knobs are inert there,
869	// so leaving them zero keeps the config surface for a P1 deployment empty.
870	func (s *SchedulerConfig) applyDefaults() {
871		if s.Policy == "" {
872			s.Policy = PolicyActiveBackup
873		}
874		if s.Policy != PolicyWeighted {
875			return
876		}
877		if s.PerPathCapacityFPS == 0 {
878			s.PerPathCapacityFPS = defaultPerPathCapacityFPS
879		}
880		if s.EngageFraction == 0 {
881			s.EngageFraction = defaultEngageFraction
882		}
883		if s.DisengageFraction == 0 {
884			s.DisengageFraction = defaultDisengageFraction
885		}
886		if s.CollapseDwell == 0 {
887			s.CollapseDwell = defaultCollapseDwell
888		}
889		if s.LoadTau == 0 {
890			s.LoadTau = defaultLoadTau
891		}
892		if s.PacingBurstFrames == 0 {
893			s.PacingBurstFrames = defaultPacingBurstFrames
894		}
895		if s.WeightRTTFloor == 0 {
896			s.WeightRTTFloor = defaultWeightRTTFloor
897		}
898		if s.WeightLossFloor == 0 {
899			s.WeightLossFloor = defaultWeightLossFloor
900		}
901	}

903	// validate enforces the scheduler policy invariants. active-backup needs no tuning.
904	// The weighted policy fails fast on any out-of-range knob so a mis-tuned
905	// aggregation policy is rejected at load rather than misbehaving at runtime (the
906	// hysteresis band, in particular, is only a band when disengage < engage).
907	func (s SchedulerConfig) validate() error {
908		if !s.Policy.valid() {
909			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.policy must be %q or %q, got %q", PolicyActiveBackup, PolicyWeighted, s.Policy)
910		}
911		if s.Policy != PolicyWeighted {
912			return nil
913		}
914		if s.PerPathCapacityFPS <= 0 {
915			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.per_path_capacity_fps must be > 0 under the weighted policy, got %g", s.PerPathCapacityFPS)
916		}
917		if s.EngageFraction <= 0 || s.EngageFraction > 1 {
918			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.engage_fraction must be in (0,1], got %g", s.EngageFraction)
919		}
920		if s.DisengageFraction < 0 || s.DisengageFraction >= s.EngageFraction {
921			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.disengage_fraction must be in [0,engage_fraction=%g) to form a hysteresis band, got %g", s.EngageFraction, s.DisengageFraction)
922		}
923		if s.CollapseDwell < 0 {
924			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.collapse_dwell must be >= 0, got %s", s.CollapseDwell)
925		}
926		if s.LoadTau <= 0 {
927			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.load_tau must be > 0, got %s", s.LoadTau)
928		}
929		if s.PacingEnabled && s.PacingBurstFrames <= 0 {
930			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.pacing_burst_frames must be > 0 when pacing is enabled, got %g", s.PacingBurstFrames)
931		}
932		if s.WeightRTTFloor <= 0 {
933			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.weight_rtt_floor must be > 0 under the weighted policy, got %s", s.WeightRTTFloor)
934		}
935		if s.WeightLossFloor <= 0 {
936			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.weight_loss_floor must be > 0 under the weighted policy, got %g", s.WeightLossFloor)
937		}
938		return nil
939	}
940	
941	// validate enforces the required-field invariants, failing on the first problem.
942	func (c *Config) validate() error {
943		if !c.Role.valid() {
944			return fmt.Errorf("role must be %q or %q, got %q", RoleEdge, RoleConcentrator, c.Role)
945		}
946		if len(c.Paths) == 0 {
947			return errors.New("at least one path is required")
948		}
949		seen := make(map[string]struct{}, len(c.Paths))
950		// seenSrc maps an already-claimed source_addr to the path that claimed it, so a
951		// second path reusing it can be rejected at LOAD naming both conflicting paths.
952		// The multipath bind Opens each path on (source_addr, listen_port), so two paths
953		// sharing a source_addr collide EADDRINUSE at the second ListenUDP (and on every
954		// re-Open after Down/Up, since the engine passes the fixed port back) — a
955		// misconfiguration that must fail fast at load, not at bring-up (defect D10).
956		seenSrc := make(map[netip.Addr]string, len(c.Paths))
957		for i, p := range c.Paths {
958			if p.Name == "" {
959				return fmt.Errorf("path %d: name is required", i)
960			}
961			if _, dup := seen[p.Name]; dup {
962				return fmt.Errorf("duplicate path name %q", p.Name)
963			}
964			seen[p.Name] = struct{}{}
965			if !p.SourceAddr.IsValid() {
966				return fmt.Errorf("path %q: source_addr is required", p.Name)
967			}
968			// Compare the UNMAPPED form: "192.0.2.10" (Is4) and "::ffff:192.0.2.10"
969			// (Is4In6) are distinct under netip == yet bind the identical v4 socket, so
970			// the two spellings must collide in the guard exactly as they do at bind.
971			src := p.SourceAddr.Unmap()
972			if prev, dup := seenSrc[src]; dup {
973				return fmt.Errorf("paths %q and %q share source_addr %s; each path must bind a distinct source address (a shared source collides EADDRINUSE at the second bind)", prev, p.Name, p.SourceAddr)
974			}
975			seenSrc[src] = p.Name
976		}
977		if !c.WireGuard.PrivateKey.IsSet() {
978			return errors.New("wireguard.private_key is required")
979		}
980		if len(c.WireGuard.Peers) == 0 {
981			return errors.New("at least one wireguard peer is required")
982		}
983		// The edge dials exactly one concentrator peer per process (Q21); multi-peer
984		// configs are concentrator-only scope. Check this before the per-peer loop
985		// below so an edge config with >1 peer is rejected with this scope-explaining
986		// message regardless of whether the peers are otherwise well-formed.
987		if c.Role == RoleEdge && len(c.WireGuard.Peers) > 1 {
988			return fmt.Errorf("edge role supports exactly one wireguard peer per process (got %d); the edge dials a single concentrator peer, multi-peer configs are concentrator-only", len(c.WireGuard.Peers))
989		}
990		for i, peer := range c.WireGuard.Peers {
991			if !peer.PublicKey.IsSet() {
992				return fmt.Errorf("wireguard peer %d: public_key is required", i)
993			}
994			// EndpointSpecs (not Endpoints) is the "any endpoint entry configured" check:
995			// a hostname-only peer contributes nothing to Endpoints at load time (Q30 —
996			// resolution is deferred to runtime) but must still count as configured.
997			if c.Role == RoleEdge && len(peer.EndpointSpecs) == 0 {
998				return fmt.Errorf("wireguard peer %d: endpoint is required for the edge role", i)
999			}
1000			// The concentrator learns the edge's endpoint dynamically (roaming across
1001			// NAT rebinds); an ordered failover endpoint list is meaningless for it, so
1002			// reject rather than silently ignore a mis-targeted config (Q18 is edge-side
1003			// only).
1004			if c.Role == RoleConcentrator && len(peer.EndpointSpecs) != 0 {
1005				return fmt.Errorf("wireguard peer %d: endpoint/endpoints is not meaningful for the concentrator role (it learns the edge's endpoint dynamically)", i)
1006			}
1007			// The DNS opt-in (Q29) is edge-only, mirroring the endpoints rule above: a
1008			// concentrator learns the edge's endpoint dynamically, so a hostname
1009			// endpoint config (and its dns = true opt-in) is meaningless there even
1010			// when no endpoint/endpoints entries are present.
1011			if c.Role == RoleConcentrator && peer.DNS {
1012				return fmt.Errorf("wireguard peer %d: dns is not meaningful for the concentrator role (it learns the edge's endpoint dynamically)", i)
1013			}
1014		}
1015		// Per-peer name/psk (Q21 multi-peer concentrator): with a single peer the
1016		// top-level Config.PSK remains the sole authenticator (T80) and a per-peer
1017		// psk is redundant, so reject one if given. With more than one peer, the
1018		// single top-level psk can no longer discriminate which peer authenticated
1019		// an inbound frame, so each peer must carry its own name and psk, both
1020		// required and pairwise-distinct — equal per-peer psks would defeat that
1021		// authenticated demux.
1022		if len(c.WireGuard.Peers) == 1 {
1023			if c.WireGuard.Peers[0].PSK.IsSet() {
1024				return errors.New("wireguard peer 0: psk is not meaningful with a single peer; the top-level psk is used as the default")
1025			}
1026		} else {
1027			seenNames := make(map[string]int, len(c.WireGuard.Peers))
1028			seenPSKs := make(map[[keyLen]byte]int, len(c.WireGuard.Peers))
1029			for i, peer := range c.WireGuard.Peers {
1030				if peer.Name == "" {
1031					return fmt.Errorf("wireguard peer %d: name is required when more than one peer is configured", i)
1032				}
1033				if prev, dup := seenNames[peer.Name]; dup {
1034					return fmt.Errorf("wireguard peers %d and %d share name %q; peer names must be unique", prev, i, peer.Name)
1035				}
1036				seenNames[peer.Name] = i
1037				if !peer.PSK.IsSet() {
1038					return fmt.Errorf("wireguard peer %d (%q): psk is required when more than one peer is configured", i, peer.Name)
1039				}
1040				if prev, dup := seenPSKs[peer.PSK.Bytes()]; dup {
1041					return fmt.Errorf("wireguard peers %d (%q) and %d (%q) share the same psk; per-peer psks must be pairwise distinct (equal psks defeat authenticated demux)", prev, c.WireGuard.Peers[prev].Name, i, peer.Name)
1042				}
1043				seenPSKs[peer.PSK.Bytes()] = i
1044			}
1045		}
1046		if c.Role == RoleConcentrator && c.WireGuard.ListenPort == 0 {
1047			return errors.New("wireguard.listen_port is required for the concentrator role")
1048		}
1049		if !c.PSK.IsSet() {
1050			return errors.New("psk is required (authenticates outer control/probe frames)")
1051		}
1052		if err := c.Amnezia.validate(); err != nil {
1053			return err
1054		}
1055		if err := c.Scheduler.validate(); err != nil {
1056			return err
1057		}
1058		if err := c.FEC.validate(); err != nil {
1059			return err
1060		}
1061		return nil
1062	}
1063	
1064	// validate enforces the amnezia obfuscation invariants (defect D1). An
1065	// unconfigured block is valid (plain WireGuard). A configured block must specify
1066	// the WHOLE junk/size set and carry a consistent magic-header set, so a partial
1067	// or inconsistent obfuscation profile FAILS FAST at load rather than producing a
1068	// silently mismatched tunnel that never handshakes.
1069	//
1070	// This runs after applyDefaults, so an omitted magic-header set has already been
1071	// filled with the standard 1..4 values; the header check below therefore only
1072	// fires for a genuinely partial header set (some given, some left zero).
1073	func (a Amnezia) validate() error {
1074		if !a.Configured() {
1075			return nil
1076		}
1077		// All-or-nothing: enabling amnezia at all requires the full junk/size set, so
1078		// both ends are forced to specify the same complete profile. A partial block
1079		// (e.g. jc/jmin/jmax set but s1/s2 omitted) would leave the ends deriving
1080		// different profiles and the handshake would fail closed with no diagnostic.
1081		if a.Jc <= 0 || a.Jmin <= 0 || a.Jmax <= 0 || a.S1 <= 0 || a.S2 <= 0 {
1082			return fmt.Errorf("amnezia: incomplete obfuscation set — when configured, jc, jmin, jmax, s1 and s2 must all be > 0 (got jc=%d jmin=%d jmax=%d s1=%d s2=%d)",
1083				a.Jc, a.Jmin, a.Jmax, a.S1, a.S2)
1084		}
1085		if a.Jmin > a.Jmax {
1086			return fmt.Errorf("amnezia: require jmin <= jmax, got jmin=%d jmax=%d", a.Jmin, a.Jmax)
1087		}
1088		// Junk sizes must not collide: amneziawg-go classifies an incoming datagram's
1089		// message type by its on-the-wire length, so the obfuscated initiation packet
1090		// (MessageInitiationSize + s1) and response packet (MessageResponseSize + s2)
1091		// must differ in length. A colliding s1/s2 pair passes config load but is
1092		// rejected later by the engine's IpcSet ("new init size == new response size");
1093		// reject it here so the collision fails fast at load, at the right locus. The
1094		// engine's own MessageInitiationSize (148) and MessageResponseSize (92)
1095		// constants are used so this check tracks the vendored fork if it ever changes.
1096		if awgdevice.MessageInitiationSize+a.S1 == awgdevice.MessageResponseSize+a.S2 {
1097			return fmt.Errorf("amnezia: junk sizes collide — obfuscated init size %d (%d+s1) must differ from response size %d (%d+s2); adjust s1/s2",
1098				awgdevice.MessageInitiationSize+a.S1, awgdevice.MessageInitiationSize,
1099				awgdevice.MessageResponseSize+a.S2, awgdevice.MessageResponseSize)
1100		}
1101		// Magic headers must be distinct: the receive path classifies a datagram's
1102		// message type by its header value, so two equal headers make two message
1103		// types indistinguishable. An all-zero header set is left for applyDefaults;
1104		// any non-zero header requires a complete, distinct set (a partial set leaves
1105		// zeros here and is caught as a duplicate).
1106		if a.H1 != 0 || a.H2 != 0 || a.H3 != 0 || a.H4 != 0 {
1107			if a.H1 == a.H2 || a.H1 == a.H3 || a.H1 == a.H4 ||
1108				a.H2 == a.H3 || a.H2 == a.H4 || a.H3 == a.H4 {
1109				return fmt.Errorf("amnezia: magic headers must be a complete, distinct set, got h1=%d h2=%d h3=%d h4=%d",
1110					a.H1, a.H2, a.H3, a.H4)
1111			}
1112		}
1113		return nil
1114	}
